nativeRange.habitat: Endemic to Australian coastal waters, ranging from southern Queensland around the southern coast to southwestern Western Australia, including Tasmania. Juveniles tend to occupy estuaries and shallow rocky reefs with seagrass; adults are found on inshore and offshore rocky reefs at depths reported between roughly 1 and 90 m.

diet.notes: In the wild the species has been documented feeding primarily on small crustaceans and worms taken from rocky-reef substrate. In captivity it is generally advised to offer a varied meaty diet of chopped marine fish flesh, scallops, shrimp/mysis, and other small crustacean preparations, supplemented occasionally with dried marine algae. Two small feedings per day is commonly recommended.

goodWith: peaceful temperate marine fish of similar size

summary: A distinctive temperate Australian reef fish with a deep, laterally-compressed body, two tall dorsal fins, and 6–8 vertical black bars on a silver-white background. Generally considered intermediate in care, with venomous dorsal spines and a strong preference for cool, rocky temperate-reef conditions.
careNotes: The Old Wife (Enoplosus armatus) is the sole living member of the family Enoplosidae and is endemic to southern Australia, found from southern Queensland around the south coast to southwestern Western Australia and Tasmania. It inhabits temperate inshore and offshore rocky reefs and is described in the wild as occurring solitarily, in pairs, or in large schools, so aquarium grouping is flexible — single specimens, pairs, or small groups can be kept provided multiples are introduced simultaneously to reduce conspecific aggression. Reported adult size varies widely between sources: LiveAquaria lists about 15 in (~38 cm), the Australian Museum reports typical adults to ~25 cm, FishBase records a maximum of 50 cm TL, and Quality Marine cites slightly over 9 in (~23 cm); a working range of 25–50 cm is documented here, with most captive specimens likely closer to the lower end. Tank-size recommendations also disagree (Quality Marine ~100 US gal / ~380 L, LiveAquaria 180 US gal / ~680 L, Aquarium Wiki 250 US gal / ~946 L); a minimum of roughly 700 L (about 185 US gal) is recommended here to give this active, sometimes schooling species adequate swim room and a long footprint over depth. As a temperate species, it generally needs cooler water than typical tropical reef tanks — a working range of about 18–24°C is documented (FishBase reports a preferred wild range closer to 15.3–20.6°C; LiveAquaria suggests roughly 18–24°C; warmer sustained temperatures should be avoided). Specific gravity is generally kept around 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12. The first dorsal-fin spines are venomous and have been compared to a bee sting in severity — handling requires caution. The species is generally considered not reef-safe: it will feed on small ornamental shrimp and small mobile invertebrates and is not recommended with stony or soft corals. Juveniles and adults differ visibly: juveniles are more elongate with blotched patterns and a white-rimmed spot on the soft dorsal fin, while adults develop the deep body, two tall sickle-like dorsal fins, and the characteristic 6–8 vertical black bars on silver. The common name is reported to derive from a tooth-grinding noise the fish makes when caught, said by early sailors to resemble the grumbling of an old wife. The species is a rare and intermittently-available specialty import in the trade.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Care sheet sourced via web search; the supplied collection URL (miscellaneous-fish) did not list this rare species but the dedicated product page was confirmed. Provides care level, tank size, pH, temperature, salinity, dKH, venomous status, and reef compatibility.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Enoplosidae, Centrarchiformes), maximum length (50 cm TL), endemicity to Australia, depth range (to 90 m), schooling/pairing behavior, venomous dorsal spines, and preferred temperature range (15.3–20.6°C, mean 17.7°C — cooler than LiveAquaria's recommendation; averaged with hobby sources to 18–24°C).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check: 100 US gal minimum, 72–78°F (warmer than LiveAquaria/FishBase), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, carnivorous diet (meaty foods twice daily), venomous dorsal spines, not reef-safe. Tank-size spread (100/180/250 gal) resolved by biasing larger to ~700 L per playbook rule for active reef fish needing swim room.

additional.name: Australian Museum
additional.notes: Authoritative range and biology: endemic from southern Queensland to southwestern Western Australia, common on coastal reefs as solitary individuals, pairs, or large schools; juveniles in estuaries and shallow seagrass/rocky-reef habitat. Typical adult size reported as ~25 cm (lower than FishBase's 50 cm maximum). Juvenile vs. adult color phase confirmed.

additional.name: The Aquarium Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary (Reef2Reef wiki) was not reachable; aquarium-wiki was used as a fourth hobby reference. Confirms 250 US gal (~946 L), 18–25°C, pH 8.0–8.5, SG 1.015–1.025, dKH 8–12, 5–12 year lifespan.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable home-aquarium breeding protocol is documented. Spawning behavior in the wild appears to be poorly studied in the published hobby literature.
